name: Data-Transfer Intent
summary: The typed-payload + accepts contract behind the native DataTransfer object shared by clipboard and drag events: a payload (text, rich content, files, app items) entering a zone that declares what it accepts, plus the copy/cut/paste copy-out half. Drop-target and file-source compose it.

flow.description: Direction of transfer across the zone — into it, out of it, or both

payload.description: The typed content category the DataTransfer carries; a zone's accepts list is expressed over these

acceptance.description: How a receiving zone constrains an incoming payload

description:
One object, two surfaces

The browser moves a payload through a single native object — DataTransfer — exposed as event.clipboardData on clipboard events and event.dataTransfer on drag events. This intent names the recurring contract that rides on it: a typed payload entering a zone that declares what it accepts, plus the programmatic copy-out half (navigator.clipboard, ClipboardItem). It is UX vocabulary only — the native objects and any provider sit behind it.

Flow

  • receive: payload moves in — a paste or a drop onto the zone.
  • emit: payload moves out — a copy or cut from the zone (the half neither drop-target nor file-source owns).
  • both: the zone accepts and offers — e.g. an editor region that pastes in and copies out.

Payload

The typed categories a transfer carries; a zone's accepts list is a subset of these, mirroring the multiple MIME entries a single DataTransfer exposes.

  • text: plain string (text/plain).
  • rich: structured/markup content (text/html, text/uri-list, and other format strings).
  • files: one or more File objects (DataTransfer.files / a pasted/dropped file).
  • items: application-defined data carried under a custom format string (a dragged row, a card, an app entity).

Acceptance

  • declared: the zone publishes an allow-list of payload categories; a payload outside it is rejected (and the rejection is surfaced, not silently dropped). The validation contract the neighbours share.
  • any: the zone takes whatever arrives and sorts it out itself.

Composition

This intent is composed, not used bare — it is the substrate its consumers share so the accepts contract is defined once:

  • Drop-target (the drag-and-drop trait family, #022) is a receive zone with a declared accepts list — it composes this rather than re-deriving payload/accepts validation.
  • File Revision's file-source paste/drop providers consume it for the clipboard-paste and drop ingestion paths.
  • The clipboard emit (copy/cut) surface rides here as the copy-out half — grounded in navigator.clipboard.write / ClipboardItem — which neither neighbour previously owned.

Interface Protocol

type Flow = 'receive' | 'emit' | 'both';
type Payload = 'text' | 'rich' | 'files' | 'items';
type Acceptance = 'declared' | 'any';

interface DataTransferIntent {
    // Into the zone (paste/drop), out of it (copy/cut), or both
    flow: Flow;
    // Typed categories the transfer may carry
    payload: Payload[];
    // Whether a receiving zone validates against a declared accepts list
    acceptance: Acceptance;
    // The declared allow-list, when acceptance is 'declared'
    accepts?: Payload[];
}

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: Clipboard and drag-and-drop share one DataTransfer object exposing typed entries by format string plus a files list — the single substrate this intent names, so the typed-payload + accepts contract is defined once across both surfaces.
The programmatic copy-out/paste-in half — ClipboardItem carries multiple typed representations, matching the payload categories — is the emit flow that drop-target and file-source do not own.

A drop zone signals acceptance via dragover/dropEffect against the dragged types — the receive + declared-accepts behavior, validating what it takes before the drop, that this intent factors out of the drop-target trait.
